one_line_takeaway: A well-structured 2026 survey that maps LLM hallucination end-to-end via lifecycle-linked cause, detection, mitigation, and explainability taxonomies, concluding no single method works and that failure-mode-diverse hybrids plus human oversight are the path forward; it is a reliability/trustworthiness survey (security-adjacent, not adversarial).
key_finding: The survey's central synthesized conclusion is that no single detection paradigm and no single mitigation technique suffices in isolation, and that complementary hybrids are the most promising direction. For detection: retrieval-based methods handle factual errors but are hostage to retrieved-document quality; learning-based methods are accurate but need high-quality annotated data; uncertainty-based methods avoid label dependence but over-flag and miss high-confidence hallucinations; self-consistency methods catch inconsistency without external evidence but fail when the model is confidently and consistently wrong and depend on prompt/sampling diversity; embedding-based methods capture semantic discrepancy but degrade out-of-domain and in low-resource languages. Crucially, the authors warn that hybrids must combine methods with DIFFERENT failure modes (e.g., retrieval + learning), since uncertainty, self-consistency, and internal-representation methods share the same blind spot for high-confidence errors. For mitigation, the strongest combinations pair prompting/reasoning with retrieval and model-centric training.
core_contribution: A comprehensive survey (Alansari & Luqman, KFUPM) of hallucination in large language models. Its OWN intellectual product is a set of taxonomies, not any experiment. It contributes: (1) a causes taxonomy explicitly linked to the six-stage LLM development lifecycle (data curation, model architecture, pre-training, fine-tuning, evaluation, inference), enumerating stage-specific mechanisms (e.g., imitative falsehoods and long-tail knowledge in data; softmax bottleneck and unidirectional contextualization in architecture; exposure bias / teacher forcing and lack of negative examples in pre-training; capability/belief misalignment and sycophancy from RLHF; inadequate metrics at evaluation; sampling randomness and reasoning limits at inference); (2) a five-category detection taxonomy (retrieval-, uncertainty-, embedding-, learning-, and self-consistency-based); (3) a four-category mitigation taxonomy (prompt-, retrieval-, reasoning-, and model-centric training/adaptation-based); (4) an explainability split into model-derived vs. grounding explainability; (5) explicit emphasis on multilingual/low-resource settings and on the newer wave of reasoning-based mitigation (CoT, iterative refinement, Chain-of-X). It also positions itself against 11 prior hallucination surveys in a feature-comparison table.
threat_model: N/A. No adversarial threat model. Hallucination as defined here (fluent, syntactically correct output that is factually inaccurate or unsupported by evidence) is an unintentional reliability/factuality failure of the model, not an attack by an adversary. The paper defines no attacker, capability, or attack surface. It notes downstream harms (misinformation, privacy breaches, code with security vulnerabilities, clinical/legal/financial risk) but treats them as consequences of unreliability, not as security threats.
limitations: (1) As a survey it produces no new empirical evidence; all numbers are re-reported from cited primary studies under heterogeneous datasets, tasks, and metrics, so cross-method comparison is not apples-to-apples (results in Table 2 mix AUROC, F1, and accuracy). (2) The authors themselves note there is no universally agreed correct explanation for a given hallucination, and no standardized benchmarks/metrics for explanation quality, hampering evaluation of explainability methods. (3) Automatic evaluation metrics (ROUGE, BLEU, BERTScore) are flagged as failing to capture factuality/faithfulness, a gap the field has not closed. (4) Coverage of the dataset and metric catalog (Sections 8-9, incl. Table 5) falls beyond the portion analyzed here. (5) The survey is descriptive/organizational rather than prescriptive: it advocates hybrid and human-in-the-loop deployment for critical domains but offers no unified quantitative meta-analysis.

datasets_used: No datasets used by the authors (survey). Benchmarks CATALOGUED/reviewed and appearing in the cited results include: WikiBio, TruthfulQA, TriviaQA, SQuAD, Natural Questions (NQ), HaluEval-QA, HaluEval-Sum, RagTruth, CNN/DM, HotpotQA (Table 2 detection benchmarks); HalluQA (JointCQ eval); FactKG (FactGenius eval).
quantitative_results: All figures below are REPORTED BY THE SURVEY from cited primary works, not produced by the survey authors.
JointCQ (detection): reported 80.58% accuracy and 83.05% F1 on HalluQA.
Chen et al. graph-enhanced uncertainty (detection): reported +19.78% Spearman correlation at passage-level detection.
DoLa (decoding mitigation): reported 12-17% improvement in truthfulness scores on TruthfulQA.
Context-aware contrastive decoding (Shi et al.): reported +14.3% factuality on a summarization task.
Sennrich et al. source-/language-contrastive decoding (MT): reported reducing severe hallucinations (chrF2<10) by 67-83% and oscillatory hallucinations by 75-92% across language pairs.
CoQ reasoning: reported reducing factual inaccuracies by 31% vs. CoT (and 38% vs. the two most prevalent LLMs).
CoN prompting: reported +7.9% average exact-match on noisy retrieved docs and +10.5% rejection rate on out-of-scope questions.
KG-Adapter (fine-tuning mitigation): reported 38% reduction in knowledge conflicts vs. prompt-based methods, using ~28M trained parameters for 7B-scale LLMs.
FactGenius (KG fact verification): reported +12% F1 on the FactKG benchmark.
Feldman et al. tagged prompting: reported a 98.88% success rate in eliminating fabricated information with context-embedded tags.
baselines_compared: The survey's OWN comparison (Table 1): this survey vs. 11 prior hallucination surveys [refs 5,8,9,11,12,13,14,15,16,17,18] across eight features F1-F8; this survey is the only one satisfying all eight.

Named prior surveys contrasted: Ji et al. (2023), Ye et al. (2023), Zhang et al. (2023), Huang et al. (2023, factuality/faithfulness taxonomy), Tonmoy et al. (2024, mitigation taxonomy), Saxena & Bhattacharyya (2024), Qi et al. (2024), Cossio (2025), Rahman et al. (2025), Malin et al. (2025).

Baselines such as GPT-4, SelfCheckGPT, RIPA, and RAG appear only INSIDE cited primary studies (e.g., FAVA reportedly outperforms GPT-4 on span-level detection; HaluAgent reportedly comparable to GPT-4), not as the survey's own experimental baselines.
